title: L02.1.primitives
body:
Toolkit for Finance and Financial Decision Making Lecture 2.1: Constructors, literals, basic types

Breno Schmidt and Robert Tumarkin UNSW

Overview

Constructors Literals Five essential types  int  float  str  bool  NoneType

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals  Review: Objects in Python

Python is an object-oriented language  Everything in Python is an object (an instance of some class) A class is a blueprint for objects  Calling a class constructs an instance  The class defines the attributes and methods shared by its instances Every instance has exactly one class  That class is the instance’s type For our purposes, an instance’s type is simply its class (the one you called).

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals

Python uses the class as a template to create instances.

You can always create an instance with the class name and parameter values in parentheses. Examples include: int(1), for integers float(1.0), for non-integer real numbers str('This is text'), for text

This approach uses constructors.

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals

Python uses the class as a template to create instances.

Fundamental classes allow you to create instances with a shorthand that requires just specifying a value. For example, 1 (creates the same object as int(1)) 1.0 (creates the same object as float(1.0)) 'This is text' (creates the same object as str('This is text'))

This approach uses literals.

Python: Creating instances with constructors and literals

Why use literals?

They are easier to read, cleaner, and shorter Python optimises literals so they are typically faster than constructors

Why use constructors?

Most of the time, you must. Every class has a constructor, while only a select few classes have literals. The resulting class is very explicit. They are not susceptible to minor typographical errors (e.g., whole number integers and floating points)

In general, use a literal instead of the constructor whenever possible.

Python: Basic building blocks  Basic building blocks

Five of Python’s essential types:

Four primitive types

int: Integers representing whole numbers exactly float: Floating points representing real numbers inexactly str: Strings of characters representing textual information bool: Boolean values representing logical True/False

One special ‘singleton’ type

NoneType: None, a single instance representing missing information

Whole numbers: Integer

Integers are whole-valued numerical data (positive, zero, and negative).

Instances are created by: A sequence of digits without a decimal point:  Python recognises 1 as an integer.  Python considers 1. and 1.0 to be floating-point numbers. A preceding negative sign indicates a negative value: Underscores may be used to group digits:  Python recognises 1_000 as an integer Commas may not be used to group digits:  Python interprets 1,000 to be a tuple (to be discussed later)

Integers are represented as instances of the int class

Real numbers: Floating point

Floating point numbers (floats) are inexact, binary representations of real-valued numbers.

Instances are created by: Two methods:  A sequence of digits followed by a decimal point with optional additional digits after the decimal point. • Python considers 1. and 1.0 to be floating-point numbers. • Python considers 1.1 to be a floating-point number.  A decimal point followed by digits. A preceding negative sign indicates a negative value: Underscores may be used to group digits:  Python recognises 1_000.1 as an integer Commas may not be used to group digits:  Python interprets 1,000.1 to be a tuple (to be discussed later)

Floating point numbers are represented as instances of the float class

Textual information: String

Strings are textual data.

Instances are created by: Text on a single line surrounded by single- or double-quotes. Text on a single or multiple line surrounded by three repeated single- or double-quotes. Use these only when appropriate. The symbol used to start the string must be used to end it. Examples: 1 string_a = "This is a my favourite sentence." 2 string_b = 'This is a my favourite sentence.' 3 string_c = """This is my favourite sentence. I have so much to say 4 that my thoughts go on to a second line. Let me tell 5 you about the time ...""" 6 string_d = '''This is my favourite sentence. I have so much to say 7 that my thoughts go on to a second line. Let me tell 8 you about the time ...'''

Strings are represented as instances of the str class

Textual information: String

Note that quotation characters can be included by either:

Starting the string with a different quotation character, e.g., starting a string with double-quotes, will let you have single-quotes inside it. 1 "When Cleese says: 'Now that's what I call a dead parrot'"

Preceding (escaping) the quotation character with a backslash: \" or \' 1 "Narrator: \"And now for something completely different \"" 2 'Narrator: \'And now for something completely different \''

True/False information: Boolean

Booleans are the logical values of true and false.

The two instances are created by: True False

Capitalisation matters. Python considers lowercase true and false to be variable names.

True and False are the only instances of the bool class

Missing information: None

NoneType is used to represent missing information.

The single instance is created by: The keyword None Capitalisation matters. Python considers none to be a variable name.

None is the only member of Python’s NoneType class

Missing information: None

None is very useful because information is often missing. Note that:

Operations between None and other basic types are not permitted. Method calls with None will yield an error.

None means that something is missing. It does not mean 0, False, or an empty string, which are instances of their respective types.

Key takeaways

Creating instances  Constructors: ClassName(...) (always available).  Literals: shorthand for fundamental classes (1, 1.0, 'text', True, None). Essential types  int: Represent whole numbers  float: Represent real numbers with a decimal point; inexact representation.  str: Represent text in quotes ("..." or '...'; triple quotes for multi-line).  bool: Represent exactly True or False (capitalisation matters)  NoneType: None, a single value meaning missing information Pitfalls to remember  None is not 0, False, or ""; don’t do arithmetic or method calls on None  1 is an int, while 1.0 and 1. are float
